flashcards.front: What is EOF in C++ file handling?
flashcards.back: EOF (End of File) is a special condition indicating that there is no more data to read from a file. It signals the program has reached the end of the file stream.

flashcards.front: What does the eof() member function return?
flashcards.back: The eof() function returns true (1) when the end-of-file bit (eofbit) has been set, indicating that a read operation has attempted to read past the end of the file.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between eof() and fail() in C++?
flashcards.back: eof() returns true when end-of-file is reached; fail() returns true when a read operation fails (due to type mismatch or EOF). fail() is more general and includes eof conditions.

flashcards.front: What does bad() function check in file streams?
flashcards.back: bad() returns true when a fatal I/O error occurs, such as disk failure or integrity problems. Unlike fail(), bad() indicates the stream is unusable.
The stream's bool conversion operator returns !fail(), so the loop continues while the stream is in a good state (not failed and not at EOF). It stops when any error occurs.

flashcards.front: What is the value of the EOF constant in C++?
flashcards.back: EOF is defined as -1 in C++ (and C). It is returned by functions like get() when they encounter the end of the file.

flashcards.front: How does get() method indicate EOF?
flashcards.back: The get() method returns the character read as int. When EOF is encountered, it returns EOF (which is -1) instead of a valid character.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between eofbit and eof() function?
flashcards.back: The eofbit flag is set when a read operation attempts to read past EOF. The eof() function simply returns the state of this flag.

flashcards.front: How do you clear EOF flags in C++?
flashcards.back: Use the clear() member function: infile.clear(). This resets all error flags (eofbit, failbit, badbit) to goodbit state.

flashcards.front: How is EOF detection different for binary files?
flashcards.back: For binary files, you cannot rely on EOF characters since any byte value is valid. You must check the return value of read() or use eof() to detect actual end of file.
good() returns true only when all bits (goodbit, eofbit, failbit, badbit) are false - meaning no errors and haven't reached EOF.

flashcards.front: How can peek() be used for EOF detection?
flashcards.back: peek() returns the next character without extracting it. If it returns EOF, the next read would encounter end of file. Use: while(infile.peek() != EOF)

flashcards.front: What is the correct way to read a file line by line until EOF?
flashcards.back: Use std::getline() in a while loop: std::string line; while(std::getline(infile, line)) { // process line } - getline returns false at EOF.

flashcards.front: What happens when you read past EOF?
flashcards.back: When reading past EOF, the failbit is set (and eofbit as well), subsequent reads fail, and the stream enters a failed state.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between text and binary mode EOF detection?
flashcards.back: In text mode, EOF may be detected by special characters or when read fails; in binary mode, you must rely on stream state functions (eof(), fail()) as no special EOF markers exist.
